## [00:00 - 03:01]

In this lesson, I'm going to go through my double spy framework process to get the best price possible from your suppliers. You've gone this far, you're going to China, you're going to Vietnam, you're talking to some suppliers and you're buying in bulk. It's very, very important for you to get the best price possible because we talk about how important margin is and every dollar count. So now let's go through the double spy framework, DSF. Basically we have two steps. The first step is to go to one supplier first and get the quote for the product you want to produce and drop it by 10% and tell the supplier that you intend to work with. Tell them that that's the target price that you want to hit and ask them to see if they can meet your target price. They might be able to, they might not be able to, but in the end you can decide what is the optimal buying price you want to buy at. The biggest tip I will give to all entrepreneurs is the following. So you know all suppliers will ask for 30% down payment before they even start doing the work for you and you can always go back and say 15 and see what they say. They might say yes and they might say no and they might say 20%. And I always encourage people to do that because cash flow is king. So it's very important to protect your cash flow. So anything is, you know, it's always better than nothing. The second tip when you're working with suppliers I highly recommend is buy yourself a flat and go to the supplier meet up in person because when you meet up in person it's always easy to get a deal done. When you're talking on the phone, on email, because of the time difference and because of the language barrier, because many, many different elements you might not get the best deal. And so that's the second recommendation I would suggest. And the last one is to maintain that relationship. You know, when it's Chinese New Year, buy the account manager, buy the boss a couple of little gifts that cost you 10, 20 bucks, you know, that goes a long way, trust me. The other thing is it doesn't work all the time, but I always encourage people to do is to ask for better payment term. So what does it mean? I always manage to get payment extension from my suppliers from the second order onwards. So the first order you never get that kind of payment term because you're building the trust. They, you know, they need to trust you and you need to trust them. And from the second order onwards, you can basically say, you know what, the other 70 percent or 80 percent, let me pay you 30 day after I receive stock.

## [03:01 - 06:02]

And if you're genuine enough to your supplier, they normally will say yes. So with this sort of stuff, you never put into contract. You meet up in person, you let them know that you are studying out this business and you are looking at this long-term relationship and you want better term. And if you're genuine enough, they will always agree to something like this, but it's very much like a gentleman agreement. It's not something you would put into a contract because as soon as you put in contract, they probably will say no. So because of the culture difference, whenever you make any request to Chinese manufacturers, for example, they tend to say yes to everything, but then they can't live up to it. It's very, very important that you have agreement in place that stating your quality standard and what happened if there's an inequality issue. So this is something that you definitely want to put into contract and agree by both parties. And as soon as you have agreement, they will take it very, very seriously. But on top of that, I highly recommend, you know, when you, when you are producing your stock or when you are about to finish production, go to the manufacturer and QA, QC yourself. Or if you have external QC partners, you can travel with them and go to the factory together and check your production. It's very important to set a policy quality policy with your supplier. And within that policy, you have to set very specific penalty if they don't follow your instruction. So like standard one would be something like, Hey, you have to include a 1% of hardware in the, in the, in the container or in the shipment for my stock so that if there's any repair I need to do, I have spare parts. The second bit is they have to understand if there's any quality issue, they have to replenish fresh stock in next shipment, or they have to compensate you for, you know, with the three times of the cost. So but I've never used any of these things because the second point I was, I'm about to tell you is that while you can be very strict with your, with your guidelines or with your agreement, you also don't want to, you know, compromise your relationship with the supplier. So it's a very fine balance between the two. So it's important to have agreement in place to, to make sure that follow the instructions. The second thing is you also want to have a good relationship with all your manufacturers. So don't push your supplier too hard because if you do, they might not be working with you and that's going to lead to a bigger problem. And second thing is you, you can always ask for fresh stock rather than cash because they, they never want to compensate cash. Stock is always better for them. And most importantly is manage your relationship with your supplier and visit them and, and

## [06:02 - 07:53]

meet up with them and talk about your plans and let them know your volume forecast. And it's just important to a talking person. Or if there's a, if there's a button that if you lost a button and you have, you have to replenish a new button for me and I'm going to, you know, install it myself. So there are a lot of different like little points you can put into the agreement. But generally factories are pretty okay with fresh stock in the next shipment.
